disease.name: Thyroid Nodule
disease.note: A discrete lesion within the thyroid gland. Common finding on clinical exam and imaging — up to 50% of adults have nodules on ultrasound. In Kenya, the prevalence is higher in iodine-deficient regions (Rift Valley, Western Kenya). More than 90% are benign. The goal of assessment is to identify the 5-10% that are malignant using clinical features, ultrasound (TIRADS), and FNA cytology (Bethesda system). Incidental thyroid nodules are increasingly found with wider use of carotid ultrasound and CT.
presentingComplaints: Lump in the front of the neck
Neck lump noticed by someone else
Incidental finding on scan for other reasons
Feeling of a lump in the throat

severityAssessment.severityLabel: Suspicious/malignant FNA (Bethesda V-VI) with lymph nodes -> SEVERE; benign FNA -> NON-SEVERE

history_questions.question: When did you first notice the neck lump?
Who found the lump — you, a doctor, or on a scan?
Has the lump grown in size since you noticed it?
Do you have any difficulty swallowing?
Do you have any difficulty breathing?
Has your voice changed or become hoarse?
Do you feel any pain or tenderness in the neck?
Do you have any lumps in your neck (glands)?
Do you have any family history of thyroid disease or thyroid cancer?
Have you had any radiation to the neck or chest area?
Do you live in an area with known iodine deficiency?
Do you use iodized salt in cooking?
Are you experiencing palpitations, heat intolerance, or tremors?
Do you feel tired, cold, or constipated?
Have you had any previous thyroid tests or ultrasound?
Have you had a needle biopsy of this nodule before?
Are you pregnant or planning pregnancy?
Have you lost weight unintentionally recently?
Do you have any other medical conditions?
Have you seen a traditional healer or used herbs for this lump?

examination_findings.finding: Palpable nodule in the thyroid gland, moves with swallowing
Fixed to surrounding tissues (suggests invasive malignancy)
Nodule moves freely with swallowing (likely benign)
Signs of hypothyroidism (if Hashimoto's with nodule)
Pemberton's sign (if large or retrosternal extension)
Vocal cord assessment — hoarse voice suggests RLN involvement

bedside_scores.name: TIRADS (Thyroid Imaging Reporting and Data System) Risk Stratification
bedside_scores.interpretation: TIRADS 1 (0): benign; TIRADS 2 (2): not suspicious; TIRADS 3 (3): low suspicion; TIRADS 4 (4-6): intermediate suspicion; TIRADS 5 (7-10): high suspicion. TIRADS 5: FNA if >1cm; TIRADS 4: FNA if >1.5cm; TIRADS 3: FNA if >2cm

subtypes.name: Benign Thyroid Nodule (Bethesda II)
subtypes.management: Clinical follow-up. No surgery needed unless symptomatic (cosmetic, compressive) or enlarging. Repeat US in 1-2 years (or sooner if growth). Surgery only for size >4cm, compressive symptoms, or patient preference.

subtypes.name: Suspicious/Malignant Nodule (Bethesda IV-VI)
subtypes.management: Thyroid lobectomy (if Bethesda IV-V, unilateral, <4cm) or total thyroidectomy (if Bethesda VI, bilateral, >4cm, nodal mets, or high-risk features). Central neck dissection if nodal involvement. Discuss with MDT.

bedside.name: Neck palpation with swallowing
bedside.indication: Assess mobility, size, consistency, lymph node survey

bedside.name: Vocal cord assessment (laryngoscopy)
bedside.indication: Pre-op baseline, detect subclinical RLN palsy

laboratory.name: TSH
laboratory.indication: First-line thyroid function — if suppressed, consider scintigraphy

laboratory.name: Free T4, T3
laboratory.indication: If TSH abnormal to confirm hyper/hypothyroidism

laboratory.name: Thyroglobulin
laboratory.indication: Not diagnostic — elevated in many thyroid conditions

laboratory.name: Calcitonin
laboratory.indication: Screening for medullary thyroid cancer — especially if family history or suspicious US

imaging.name: Thyroid Ultrasound (high-resolution, TIRADS classification)
imaging.indication: First-line imaging — characterize nodule, assess risk, guide FNA

imaging.name: FNA with Bethesda Cytology Classification
imaging.indication: Gold standard for cytological diagnosis — decision tool for surgery

imaging.name: Thyroid Scintigraphy (Tc-99m pertechnetate)
imaging.indication: If TSH suppressed — hot nodules are rarely malignant

imaging.name: CT Neck without contrast (or with contrast if surgery planned)
imaging.indication: If large nodule >4cm, compressive symptoms, retrosternal extension, or lymphadenopathy

management.protocolReference: ATA Guidelines for Thyroid Nodule Management (2023)

conservative: All patients with thyroid nodules undergo TSH and thyroid US with TIRADS scoring
FNA is performed based on TIRADS score and nodule size
For benign nodules (Bethesda II): reassure, no surgery needed unless symptomatic
For low-risk microPTC (<1cm, no suspicious features): active surveillance possible

surgical: Thyroid lobectomy (isthmusectomy) for Bethesda IV (follicular neoplasm), Bethesda V (suspicious), Bethesda VI if small unilateral
Total thyroidectomy for Bethesda VI (malignant), bilateral nodules requiring surgery, >4cm, or with lymph node metastases
Central neck dissection if malignant with nodal concern
nonOperative: Observation with serial US for benign nodules (Bethesda II)
Repeat FNA if Bethesda I (non-diagnostic) or Bethesda III (atypia)
Molecular testing (ThyroSeq, Afirma) may help risk-stratify Bethesda III/IV nodules
Radiofrequency ablation (RFA) for benign symptomatic nodules — alternative to surgery

immediate: Same as post-thyroidectomy care — monitor airway, voice, calcium
If lobectomy: thyroid function monitored — may need levothyroxine if TSH rises
day3_7: Final histology results — confirm benign vs malignant
If malignant: ATA risk stratification, MDT discussion, plan completion thyroidectomy if needed

pathways.treatment: Observation. Repeat US in 1-2 years. No intervention needed. Reassure and discharge if stable.

pathways.criteria: atypia of undetermined significance or follicular neoplasm, inconclusive FNA
pathways.treatment: Repeat FNA or molecular testing (ThyroSeq/Afirma). Diagnostic thyroid lobectomy if still indeterminate or high clinical suspicion. Based on results and risk factors.

pathways.criteria: suspicious for or diagnostic of malignancy on FNA
pathways.treatment: Thyroid lobectomy (for low-risk, unifocal, <4cm, unilateral) or total thyroidectomy (for high-risk, bilateral, >4cm, nodal mets, aggressive subtype). Central neck dissection if nodal involvement. MDT discussion.

operative_templates.indication: Bethesda IV (follicular neoplasm), Bethesda V (suspicious), Bethesda VI (small unilateral PTC <4cm), symptomatic large benign nodule
operative_templates.incision: Collar incision centered on the side of the nodule or in a skin crease 2cm above sternal notch
steps: Collar incision, subplatysmal flaps, divide strap muscles in midline
Identify and expose the affected thyroid lobe
Identify RLN on the affected side — dissect and protect throughout
Identify and preserve parathyroid glands on the affected side
Ligate superior pole vessels close to thyroid
Hemostasis, drain optional, close in layers

watchFor: fatigue, weight gain, elevated TSH at 6 weeks

dischargeCriteria: Wound healing, no signs of infection or hematoma
Voice normal or acceptable mild change
Histology results reviewed with patient (or pending, with follow-up arranged)
If lobectomy: no calcium issues expected
Follow-up for histology results and TSH check scheduled

patient_education.explanation: A thyroid nodule is a lump that forms in the thyroid gland in your neck. More than 90% of thyroid nodules are benign (non-cancerous). We assess each nodule using ultrasound (looking at its features) and sometimes a needle biopsy (FNA) to check the cells. Most nodules do not need any treatment — they just need to be watched. If the nodule is suspicious or causing symptoms, we may recommend surgery to remove it.
warningSigns: Rapid growth of the neck lump over weeks
New lump in the neck (lymph node)
activity: No restrictions for benign nodules under observation
After surgery: gentle neck exercises, avoid heavy lifting for 2 weeks
diet: No special diet needed for benign nodules
After surgery: normal diet as tolerated
medication: No medication needed for benign nodules
If lobectomy: thyroid function checked at 6 weeks — you may need Levothyroxine if TSH rises
If total thyroidectomy: you need Levothyroxine for life
followUp: If benign nodule under observation: repeat ultrasound in 1-2 years
If FNA was benign and nodule stable: discharged from most follow-up
If surgery was done: follow-up for histology results in 1 week
Return immediately if the nodule grows rapidly or you develop new symptoms

before_discharge: Discuss FNA results and Bethesda classification
If benign nodule under observation: schedule repeat US in 1-2 years
If surgery planned: pre-op counseling, voice assessment, calcium check

after_discharge.action: Histology results review — if surgery done, discuss final diagnosis
TSH check (if lobectomy or total thyroidectomy)
Repeat thyroid US for benign nodules under observation
Second follow-up US if stable — discharge if unchanged

when_to_return: Neck lump grows rapidly (noticeable over 2-4 weeks)
New neck lumps or swollen glands appear

pathophysiologyDetail: Thyroid nodules arise from the follicular epithelial cells lining the thyroid follicles; they represent a spectrum from benign hyperplasia to malignant transformation

Hyperplastic nodules develop in response to TSH stimulation (iodine deficiency, goitrogens) or local growth factors — they are polyclonal and composed of normal-appearing follicles filled with colloid

Colloid nodules are the most common type: they result from cycles of hyperplasia and involution, with accumulation of colloid in dilated follicles and variable degenerative changes (hemorrhage, cyst formation, fibrosis)

True follicular adenomas are monoclonal neoplasms with a well-defined fibrous capsule; they show a higher degree of cellularity and architectural atypia than hyperplastic nodules but lack capsular or vascular invasion

Somatic mutations drive neoplastic transformation: activating mutations in the TSHR gene (constitutive cAMP signaling) cause hyperfunctioning adenomas that suppress TSH — these are almost never malignant

Malignant transformation involves accumulation of driver mutations (BRAF V600E in PTC, RAS in FTC, RET in MTC) with progression through increasingly dysplastic stages — but most thyroid nodules never undergo this transformation

Cystic degeneration occurs through central necrosis and hemorrhage within nodules; the wall is composed of compressed thyroid tissue and granulation tissue. These cysts can refill after aspiration and may recur
Thyroid nodules in iodine-deficient regions (Rift Valley, Western Kenya) are more often hyperplastic/colloid nodules related to chronic TSH stimulation from long-standing iodine deficiency
The vast majority (90-95%) of thyroid nodules are benign and remain benign. The 5-10% that are malignant are predominantly papillary carcinoma (80%), which has excellent outcomes
Spongiform appearance (multiple microcystic spaces in a nodule) is a benign pattern with >99% negative predictive value for malignancy

In Kenyan patients, large, long-standing goiters may harbor dominant nodules that represent the background hyperplastic process rather than true neoplasms — but any dominant nodule with suspicious features requires FNA regardless of background goiter

landmarkTrials.trial: Marqusee E, et al. TIRADS validation — a prospective study of US features and malignancy risk
landmarkTrials.population: 350 thyroid nodules undergoing US and FNA at a single institution
landmarkTrials.finding: Microcalcifications (OR 4.7), irregular margins (OR 3.3), taller-than-wide shape (OR 10.2), and marked hypoechogenicity (OR 6.3) were independent predictors of malignancy. Combination of features gave >85% PPV for malignancy
landmarkTrials.impact: Validated the sonographic features that form the basis of the TIRADS classification system
landmarkTrials.reference: Marqusee E, et al. J Clin Endocrinol Metab. 2000;85(4):1446-1452

landmarkTrials.trial: Cibas ES, Ali SZ. The Bethesda System for Reporting Thyroid Cytopathology
landmarkTrials.population: Consensus conference of 40 experts from 18 countries reviewing thyroid FNA literature (n=30,000+ cases)
landmarkTrials.finding: Bethesda categories: II (benign) — 0-3% malignancy risk. III (AUS) — 5-15%. IV (follicular) — 15-30%. V (suspicious) — 60-75%. VI (malignant) — 97-99%
landmarkTrials.impact: Standardized thyroid cytology reporting worldwide, enabling consistent clinical decision-making and research
landmarkTrials.reference: Cibas ES, Ali SZ. The Bethesda System. Am J Clin Pathol. 2009;132(5):658-665

landmarkTrials.trial: Gharib H, et al. AACE/ACE/AME Guidelines for Thyroid Nodule Management — update 2016
landmarkTrials.population: Evidence-based guideline incorporating meta-analyses of nodule risk stratification (TIRADS) and FNA outcomes
landmarkTrials.finding: TIRADS-based FNA thresholds reduced unnecessary FNA by 50% while maintaining >95% sensitivity for malignancy. Only 5-15% of nodules require FNA based on sonographic criteria
landmarkTrials.impact: Established TIRADS as the standard for nodule risk stratification, reducing unnecessary invasive procedures
landmarkTrials.reference: Gharib H, et al. Endocr Pract. 2016;22(5):618-644

landmarkTrials.trial: Steward DL, et al. Afirma Gene Expression Classifier for Bethesda III-IV nodules
landmarkTrials.population: 273 indeterminate thyroid nodules (Bethesda III-IV) tested with the Afirma GSC before surgery
landmarkTrials.finding: Afirma GSC reclassified 54% of Bethesda III and 73% of Bethesda IV nodules as benign (molecular 'rule-out'), with 96% NPV. Cancer prevalence in 'benign' GSC results was 4% at surgery
landmarkTrials.impact: Molecular testing reduces unnecessary diagnostic surgery by 50-70% in patients with indeterminate cytology

landmarkTrials.trial: Durante C, et al. Natural history of benign thyroid nodules — a 5-year prospective study
landmarkTrials.population: 992 patients with 1,312 benign thyroid nodules followed with annual US for 5 years
landmarkTrials.finding: 85% of benign nodules were stable or decreased in size. Only 15% grew (>20% increase in 2 dimensions). Malignancy developed in only 0.3% during follow-up. Nodule growth alone was not a reliable predictor of malignancy
landmarkTrials.impact: Confirmed that most benign nodules remain benign and that surveillance imaging can be safely spaced to 1-2 years

landmarkTrials.trial: Haugen BR, et al. ATA Guidelines for thyroid nodules and differentiated thyroid cancer (2015 update)
landmarkTrials.population: Systematic review and expert consensus incorporating contemporary TIRADS, Bethesda, and molecular testing data
landmarkTrials.finding: Recommendations: TIRADS-based FNA thresholds (5/1, 4/1.5, 3/2). Active surveillance for microPTC. Lobectomy for Bethesda IV. Molecular testing for Bethesda III-IV. Total thyroidectomy for cancer >4cm or with nodal mets
landmarkTrials.impact: The most widely-used international guideline for thyroid nodule management, updated regularly

landmarkTrials.trial: Bernardi D, et al. Radiofrequency ablation vs surgery for benign thyroid nodules — randomized trial
landmarkTrials.population: 200 patients with benign compressive thyroid nodules randomized to RFA vs surgery
landmarkTrials.finding: RFA: 80% volume reduction at 1 year, no hospitalization, no hypothyroidism, no hypoparathyroidism. Surgery: 100% volume reduction, 2-day hospitalization, 50% needed thyroxine, 5% had transient hypocalcemia. Symptom improvement was comparable
landmarkTrials.impact: RFA is a safe, effective alternative to surgery for benign compressive nodules, preserving thyroid function

evidenceTable.recommendation: Thyroid ultrasound with TIRADS classification is the first-line imaging modality for all thyroid nodules
evidenceTable.reference: ATA Guidelines; AACE Guidelines; Marqusee E, J Clin Endocrinol Metab 2000

evidenceTable.recommendation: FNA is indicated based on TIRADS and nodule size: TIRADS 5 >1cm, TIRADS 4 >1.5cm, TIRADS 3 >2cm
evidenceTable.reference: ATA 2015 Guidelines; Gharib H, Endocr Pract 2016

evidenceTable.recommendation: Bethesda II (benign) nodules can be managed with observation and repeat US in 1-2 years
Bethesda III (AUS) nodules should undergo repeat FNA, molecular testing (ThyroSeq/Afirma), or diagnostic lobectomy based on clinical risk
evidenceTable.reference: Steward DL, Thyroid 2019; Cibas ES, Am J Clin Pathol 2009

evidenceTable.recommendation: Bethesda IV (follicular neoplasm) warrants diagnostic thyroid lobectomy for definitive histology
Bethesda V-VI (suspicious/malignant) warrants therapeutic thyroidectomy with appropriate lymph node dissection
Nodule growth alone (>20% increase) is not a reliable predictor of malignancy and should not be the sole indication for FNA
Calcitonin screening should be considered in all patients with thyroid nodules (especially if family history or suspicious US features)
Suppressed TSH with a thyroid nodule should prompt scintigraphy to rule out a hyperfunctioning ('hot') nodule, which is rarely malignant
Radiofrequency ablation is an alternative to surgery for benign, symptomatic, compressive thyroid nodules

disease.diagnosticAlgorithm: Thyroid nodule discovered (clinical exam or imaging) → TSH + high-resolution thyroid US with TIRADS scoring → If TSH suppressed: perform thyroid scintigraphy. If hot nodule → management for hyperthyroidism (RAI or surgery), rarely malignant. If cold nodule → continue FNA algorithm. → Apply TIRADS-based FNA thresholds: TIRADS 5 (high suspicion) → FNA if >1cm. TIRADS 4 (intermediate) → FNA if >1.5cm. TIRADS 3 (low suspicion) → FNA if >2cm. TIRADS 2 (not suspicious) → no FNA. TIRADS 1 (benign) → no FNA. → FNA with Bethesda classification: Bethesda II (benign) → observe with US in 1-2 years. Bethesda III (AUS) → repeat FNA, consider molecular testing (ThyroSeq/Afirma), or proceed to diagnostic lobectomy if high clinical suspicion. Bethesda IV (follicular neoplasm) → diagnostic lobectomy. Bethesda V (suspicious) → lobectomy or total thyroidectomy depending on risk factors. Bethesda VI (malignant) → therapeutic thyroidectomy with appropriate node dissection. → If patient is pregnant: FNA is safe during pregnancy. Surgery deferred to second trimester or postpartum. → If nodule is >4cm with compressive symptoms: consider surgery regardless of FNA result. → Follow-up: benign nodules → US in 12-24 months, then discharge if stable. If growing or new suspicious features → repeat FNA. Post-lobectomy: TSH at 6-8 weeks. If total thyroidectomy: TSH suppression + Tg surveillance.

managementPearls.context: TIRADS is only as good as the sonographer
managementPearls.pearl: TIRADS scoring depends on high-quality ultrasound and experienced interpretation. Microcalcifications are the strongest single predictor of malignancy but are easily confused with colloid particles (which have a comet-tail artifact). If in doubt about the image quality or interpretation, repeat the scan or refer to a dedicated thyroid radiology center. A poorly-performed US can miss 15% of cancers.
managementPearls.evidence: ATA Guidelines; Marqusee E, J Clin Endocrinol Metab 2000

managementPearls.context: The 'spongiform' nodule is almost always benign
managementPearls.pearl: A spongiform appearance (multiple microcystic spaces occupying >50% of the nodule) has a 99.7% negative predictive value for malignancy. If the nodule is spongiform, you can confidently avoid FNA regardless of size. This is the one US pattern that is essentially diagnostic of benignity.
managementPearls.evidence: ATA Guidelines; Gharib H, Endocr Pract 2016

managementPearls.context: Calcitonin screening in all thyroid nodules?
managementPearls.pearl: The ATA does not mandate routine calcitonin screening for thyroid nodules, but the European Thyroid Association recommends it. In Kenya, where MTC incidence may be higher due to consanguinity (increasing RET carrier frequency), I recommend a single random calcitonin for all patients with thyroid nodules. A level >100 pg/mL is highly suspicious for MTC. Caution: renal failure, proton pump inhibitors, and alcohol can elevate calcitonin.

managementPearls.context: Large nodule, benign FNA — can we observe?
managementPearls.pearl: Nodules >4cm with benign FNA are 3-5x more likely to have a false-negative FNA than smaller nodules. Consider diagnostic lobectomy for nodules >4cm even with Bethesda II cytology, especially if the patient has compressive symptoms, the nodule is growing, or there are suspicious US features. The risk of missing a malignancy is 5-10% in this group.

Thyroid 'incidentalomas' on CT/MRI are found in 15-20% of scans. If the nodule is >1cm: do a dedicated thyroid US with TIRADS. Do NOT biopsy based on CT findings alone (CT cannot distinguish benign from malignant nodules). If <1cm and no suspicious features: no further workup. The vast majority are benign.
managementPearls.evidence: ATA Guidelines; ACR Incidental Findings Committee

managementPearls.context: Multinodular goiter — which nodule to biopsy?
managementPearls.pearl: In a multinodular gland, the risk of malignancy is the same as in a solitary nodule — do not be falsely reassured. Biopsy the dominant nodule AND any nodule with suspicious US features (microcalcifications, taller-than-wide, irregular margins, marked hypoechogenicity). Even if the dominant nodule is benign, a smaller 'suspicious' nodule may be the cancer.
managementPearls.evidence: ATA Guidelines; Marqusee E, J Clin Endocrinol Metab 2000

operativeNuances.pitfall: Not carefully checking the contralateral lobe before closing — missing an incidental contralateral nodule that requires bilateral surgery
operativeNuances.expertTip: After completing the lobectomy, ALWAYS examine the contralateral lobe manually and with intraoperative US if available. If a suspicious contralateral nodule is found, obtain intraoperative frozen section or plan a completion thyroidectomy after final histology. Better to find it on the table than on next week's histology report.

operativeNuances.pitfall: Incomplete resection of the isthmus — leaving a palpable ridge of thyroid tissue that the patient can feel and worry about
operativeNuances.expertTip: When performing a lobectomy, divide the isthmus cleanly at the midline. Use bipolar diathermy or Ligasure to seal the isthmic vessels. The resected surface should be smooth and hemostatic. If you leave a bulky isthmus, the patient will feel a 'new lump' post-operatively and worry the nodule has returned.

operativeNuances.procedure: Decision to Convert Lobectomy to Total Thyroidectomy
operativeNuances.pitfall: Frozen section diagnosis of 'follicular neoplasm' leading to unnecessary total thyroidectomy — frozen section cannot diagnose capsular invasion, so it cannot distinguish adenoma from carcinoma
operativeNuances.expertTip: Frozen section for follicular neoplasms is NOT reliable — it cannot assess capsular or vascular invasion, which requires permanent sections with evaluation of the entire capsule. If frozen section is requested, it will typically return 'follicular neoplasm, cannot exclude carcinoma'. This does NOT justify proceeding to total thyroidectomy. The correct approach is diagnostic lobectomy, wait for permanent histology, then discuss completion if FTC confirmed.

operativeNuances.procedure: Completion Thyroidectomy After Diagnostic Lobectomy Reveals Cancer
operativeNuances.pitfall: Re-operative surgery in a scarred field with distorted anatomy, 5-10x higher complication rates
operativeNuances.expertTip: Completion thyroidectomy should ideally be done within 3-7 days of the initial lobectomy, before dense fibrosis develops. If delayed beyond 3 weeks, wait 3 months for scar tissue to mature and soften. Use a lateral approach (dissect on prevertebral fascia, find the RLN at the cricothyroid) to avoid the central scar. Always use nerve monitoring. Consider referral to a high-volume thyroid surgeon.

prognosis.mortality: Benign thyroid nodules: zero attributable mortality. The risk of a benign nodule being found to harbor cancer on follow-up is <0.3% over 5 years. Incidental microPTC found in lobectomy specimens: 99.7% 20-year disease-specific survival. The mortality of thyroid lobectomy surgery itself: <0.1% in experienced hands.
prognosis.morbidity: Thyroid lobectomy: transient hypoparathyroidism <1%, permanent <0.5%. Transient RLN injury 1-2%, permanent <0.5%. Neck hematoma 0.3%. Wound infection 0.5%. Hypothyroidism after lobectomy: 15-30% (higher if pre-op TSH >2.5, background thyroiditis, or large resection). Most patients remain euthyroid and do NOT need thyroxine. Scar: 2-4cm, heals well in most but keloid in 10-20% of African patients.
prognosis.recurrence: Benign nodules: 15% grow on follow-up over 5 years. Of those that grow, <1% are malignant. Cyst recurrence after aspiration: 50-80% at 1 year if not completely drained or if the cyst is complex. Post-lobectomy recurrence in the remaining lobe: 5-10% over 10 years (usually benign hyperplasia). Malignant upgrade rate: Bethesda II → malignancy <3%; Bethesda III → 10-20%; Bethesda IV → 20-40%.
prognosis.survival: Normal life expectancy for patients with benign thyroid nodules. The psychological morbidity of living with 'a lump in my neck' should not be underestimated — some patients benefit from diagnostic excision even for benign nodules to alleviate cancer anxiety. In Kenya, nodule-related anxiety is amplified by limited access to quality care in rural areas and fear of cancer.
prognosis.reference: Durante C, JAMA 2015; Cibas ES, Am J Clin Pathol 2009; Haugen BR, Thyroid 2016
